questions.question: Prove by induction that 1+2+3+...+n = n(n+1)/2 for all natural numbers n.
keyPoints: Base case: verify for n=1: 1 = 1(2)/2 = 1
Inductive hypothesis: assume true for n=k: 1+...+k = k(k+1)/2
Inductive step: prove for n=k+1
Add (k+1) to both sides: k(k+1)/2 + (k+1) = (k+1)(k+2)/2
Conclude P(n) true for all n by induction

questions.question: Prove by induction that 1³+2³+...+n³ = [n(n+1)/2]² for all n ≥ 1.
keyPoints: State P(n): sum of cubes = [n(n+1)/2]²
Base case: verify for n=1: 1³ = 1, RHS = [1(2)/2]² = 1
Inductive hypothesis: assume true for n=k
Inductive step: sum to (k+1) equals [k(k+1)/2]² + (k+1)³
Simplify to [(k+1)(k+2)/2]²
Complete proof and conclusion

questions.question: Prove by induction that 7ⁿ - 1 is divisible by 6 for all natural numbers n.
keyPoints: State P(n): 7ⁿ - 1 is divisible by 6
Base case n=1: 7-1=6, divisible by 6
Inductive hypothesis: assume 7ᵏ - 1 = 6m for some integer m
Thus divisible by 6
Conclude true for all n

questions.question: Explain the difference between weak induction and strong induction with an example.
keyPoints: Weak induction: assume P(k) true → prove P(k+1)
Strong induction: assume P(1),P(2),...,P(k) true → prove P(k+1)
Example requiring strong: Prove every integer > 1 has prime factorization
Base case: n=2 is prime
Inductive step: if n is prime done; if not, n=ab where a,b<n, use hypothesis on a and b
Conclusion: both are logically equivalent but strong is useful when multiple previous cases needed

questions.question: Prove that n! > 2ⁿ for all n ≥ 4 using mathematical induction.
keyPoints: State P(n): n! > 2ⁿ for n ≥ 4
Base case: verify for n=4: 4! = 24 > 16 = 2⁴
Inductive hypothesis: assume k! > 2ᵏ for some k ≥ 4
Inductive step: (k+1)! = (k+1)·k! > (k+1)·2ᵏ ≥ 5·2ᵏ > 2·2ᵏ = 2ᵏ⁺¹ (since k+1 ≥ 5)
Thus (k+1)! > 2ᵏ⁺¹
Conclude true for all n ≥ 4

questions.question: Define the Well-Ordering Principle and explain its equivalence to mathematical induction.
keyPoints: Well-Ordering: Every non-empty set of natural numbers has a least element
Equivalence: Both principles imply each other
To prove WOP from induction: assume no least element exists, derive contradiction
To prove induction from WOP: assume P(1) true, P(k+1) follows from P(k), let S be set of naturals where P fails, if S is empty done, if not empty WOP gives least element contradiction
Conclusion: Both are fundamental equivalent properties of natural numbers

questions.question: Using induction, prove that the recursive algorithm for Fibonacci runs in O(2ⁿ) time.
keyPoints: Define T(n) as time for Fibonacci(n)
Base: T(1)=T(2)=O(1) (constant)
Recursive case: T(n)=T(n-1)+T(n-2)+O(1)
Inductive hypothesis: T(k) ≤ c·2ᵏ for some constant c
Inductive step: T(k+1) ≤ c·2ᵏ⁻¹ + c·2ᵏ⁻² + O(1) ≤ c·(2ᵏ⁻¹+2ᵏ⁻²) + O(1) < c·2ᵏ
Therefore T(n) = O(2ⁿ)
Note: This is exponential time, hence inefficient

questions.question:
Prove the following using mathematical induction:
(a) Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ i² = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6
(b) 2ⁿ > n² for n ≥ 5
(c) n³ + 2n is divisible by 3 for all n ≥ 1
keyPoints:
For (a): Base n=1: 1 = 1(2)(3)/6 = 1; IH: assume true for k; prove for k+1 by adding (k+1)² and simplifying to get formula
For (b): Base n=5: 32 > 25; IH: assume 2ᵏ > k²; prove 2ᵏ⁺¹ > (k+1)² using 2ᵏ > k² and showing (k+1)² < 2k² for k≥5
For (c): Base n=1: 1+2=3 divisible by 3; IH: assume k³+2k = 3m; prove (k+1)³+2(k+1) = (k³+3k²+3k+1)+2k+2 = k³+2k+3(k²+k+1) = 3m+3(k²+k+1) = 3(m+k²+k+1) divisible by 3
Conclude all proofs

questions.question: Prove by strong induction that every positive integer n > 1 can be expressed as a product of prime numbers.
keyPoints: State P(n): n can be written as product of primes
Base case: n=2 is prime, so product of one prime
Inductive hypothesis: assume all integers from 2 to k can be expressed as product of primes

Inductive step: consider k+1
Case 1: k+1 is prime → already product of primes
Case 2: k+1 is composite → k+1 = ab where 2 ≤ a,b ≤ k
By hypothesis, a and b can be expressed as products of primes
Therefore k+1 = ab is also a product of primes
Conclusion: by strong induction, all integers > 1 have prime factorization

questions.question: Let a₁=2, aₙ₊₁ = 3aₙ - 2. Prove by induction that aₙ = 2·3ⁿ⁻¹ + 1 for all n ≥ 1.
keyPoints: State formula: aₙ = 2·3ⁿ⁻¹ + 1

Base case n=1: a₁ = 2, RHS = 2·3⁰ + 1 = 2·1 + 1 = 3 ≠ 2. Correction: formula should be aₙ = 2·3ⁿ⁻¹ or base check: for n=1, 2·3⁰ = 2 ✓
Actually, let's recalculate: a₁ = 2; a₂ = 3(2)-2 = 4; formula gives 2·3¹ = 6 ≠ 4
Correct formula: aₙ = 3ⁿ + 1? Check: a₁ = 3+1=4 ≠2
Correct: aₙ = 3ⁿ⁻¹ + 1? a₁ = 3⁰+1 = 2 ✓; a₂ = 3¹+1 = 4 ✓
Base case: verify for n=1
Inductive hypothesis: assume aₖ = 3ᵏ⁻¹ + 1
Inductive step: aₖ₊₁ = 3aₖ - 2 = 3(3ᵏ⁻¹ + 1) - 2 = 3ᵏ + 3 - 2 = 3ᵏ + 1 = 3⁽ᵏ⁺¹⁾⁻¹ + 1
Conclusion: formula holds for all n

questions.question: Use mathematical induction to prove that the recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n with T(1) = 1 has solution T(n) = n log₂n + n.
keyPoints: State the closed-form solution to prove
Inductive hypothesis: assume T(k) = k·log₂k + k for power of 2 k
Inductive step: consider n=2k (next power of 2)
T(2k) = 2T(k) + 2k = 2(k·log₂k + k) + 2k = 2k·log₂k + 2k + 2k = 2k·log₂k + 4k
Need to show equals 2k·log₂(2k) + 2k = 2k(log₂k + 1) + 2k = 2k·log₂k + 2k + 2k = 2k·log₂k + 4k ✓
Conclude T(n) = n·log₂n + n for all powers of 2
